  sequence and connecting map for a short exact sequence of homological complexes in an abelian
  category [Mathlib, verified this iter]. THIS is the connecting-map primitive; it lives at the

4. **Add a declaration block for the horseshoe lift** (the to-build sub-gap): given

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33

35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G := G.\mathrm{rightDerived}\,n : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) for
33	the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\) (the Mathlib anchor
36	\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is left
37	exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(anchor \ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Mathlib dependency anchors}
42	
43	The development stands on three results that Mathlib already provides for the
44	injective-resolution construction of \(R^n G\). They are recorded here as
45	first-class blocks so that the route's reliance on Mathlib is explicit and the
46	dependency graph resolves.
47	
48	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
49	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
50	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
51	  \mathlibok
52	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
53	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
54	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
55	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
56	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
57	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
58	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
59	  \[
60	    (R^n G)(X)
61	      \;\cong\;
62	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
63	  \]
64	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
65	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
66	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
67	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
68	\end{lemma}
69	
70	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
71	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
72	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
73	  \mathlibok
74	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
75	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
76	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
77	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
78	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
79	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
80	\end{lemma}
81	
82	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
83	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
84	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
88	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
89	  \[
90	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
91	  \]
92	\end{lemma}

94	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
95	
96	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
97	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
98	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
99	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
100	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
101	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
102	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
103	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
104	  % L5594--5617).
105	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
106	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
107	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
108	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
109	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
110	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
111	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
112	  % between abelian categories and assume
113	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
114	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
115	  % \begin{enumerate}
116	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
117	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
118	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
119	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
120	  % \end{enumerate}"
121	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
122	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
123	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
124	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
125	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
126	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
127	  \[
128	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
129	  \]
130	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
131	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
132	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
133	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
134	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
135	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
136	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
137	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
138	
139	  \emph{Remark (alternative shapes).} The Mathlib-idiomatic phrasing
140	  of ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\)'' is ``\(\mathrm{IsZero}\,((R^k G).\mathrm{obj}\,J)\)''
141	  using the \texttt{Limits.IsZero} predicate rather than an equality to a chosen
142	  zero object. Two equivalent quantifier shapes are available: ``for all \(k \geq
143	  1\)'' (a hypothesis \(1 \le k\)) or the index-shifted ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J)\) is zero
144	  for all \(k\)''; the latter avoids the inequality side-condition and matches
145	  Mathlib's \texttt{isZero\_rightDerived\_obj\_injective\_succ}
146	  (anchor~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), so it is the recommended
147	  definitional form.
148	\end{definition}

150	\section{The dimension-shift step}
151	
152	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
153	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
154	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. We isolate it as a sub-lemma.
155	
156	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
157	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
158	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
159	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
160	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
161	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
162	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
164	  % between abelian categories and assume
165	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
166	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
167	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
168	  % \begin{enumerate}
169	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
170	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
171	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
172	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
173	  % \end{enumerate}
174	  % In each of the three cases
175	  % $$
176	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
177	  % $$
178	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
179	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
180	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
181	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
182	  \[
183	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
184	  \]
185	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
186	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
187	  \begin{enumerate}
188	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
189	      furnish isomorphisms
190	      \[
191	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
192	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
193	      \]
194	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
195	      \[
196	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
197	      \]
198	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
199	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
200	  \end{enumerate}
201	\end{lemma}
202	
203	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By
204	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}
205	% we obtain a distinguished triangle
206	% $(A[0], B[0], C[0], a, b, c)$ in $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
207	% As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
208	% $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
209	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
210	% we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
211	% $$
212	% 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots
213	% $$
214	% in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$. Thus the lemma follows from
215	% the characterization of acyclic objects in
216	% Lemma \ref{lemma-F-acyclic}."
217	\begin{proof}
218	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
219	  The short exact sequence \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) induces, via the
220	  \(\delta\)-functor (long exact sequence) structure of the right-derived functors
221	  \(\{R^k G\}_{k \geq 0}\), the long exact cohomology sequence in \(\mathcal{B}\)
222	  \[
223	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
224	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to (R^1 G)(Z)
225	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^2 G)(A) \to \cdots,
226	  \]
227	  where the identifications \(R^0 G(A) = G(A)\), etc., in the first row use the
228	  left-exactness of \(G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}). Because
229	  \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term
230	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\) vanishes. The segment of the long exact sequence
231	  around \((R^k G)(J)\) for \(k \geq 1\),
232	  \[
233	    (R^{k} G)(J) \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
234	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J),
235	  \]
236	  then has its two outer terms zero, so the connecting map
237	  \(\delta : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
238	  In the lowest degree the segment
239	  \[
240	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) = 0
241	  \]
242	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta\) is an epimorphism with kernel the
243	  image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta\) identifies the cokernel of
244	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is part (2).
245	\end{proof}

247	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
248	
249	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
250	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
251	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
252	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
253	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
254	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
255	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
256	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
257	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
258	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
259	  % L5785--5811).
260	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
261	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
262	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
263	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
264	  % $$
265	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
266	  % $$
267	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
268	  % $RF$."
269	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
270	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
271	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
272	  % and we obtain an exact functor
273	  % $$
274	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
275	  % $$
276	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
277	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
278	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
279	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
280	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
281	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
282	  Let
283	  \[
284	    0 \longrightarrow A
285	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
286	      \longrightarrow \cdots
287	  \]
288	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
289	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
290	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
291	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
292	  \[
293	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
294	  \]
295	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
296	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
297	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
298	  object.
299	\end{theorem}
300	
301	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
302	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
303	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
304	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
305	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
306	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
307	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
308	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
309	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
310	% the long exact cohomology sequence
311	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
312	% to an exact sequence
313	% $$
314	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
315	% $$
316	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
317	\begin{proof}
318	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
319	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
320	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
321	  The argument is the classical dimension shift. Decompose the resolution into the
322	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
323	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
324	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
325	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
326	  \[
327	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
328	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
329	  \]
330	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
331	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
332	
333	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
334	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
335	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
336	  \[
337	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
338	      = (R^0 G)(A),
339	  \]
340	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
341	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
342	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
343	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
344	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
345	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
346	  \[
347	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
348	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
349	  \]
350	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
351	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
352	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
353	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
354	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
355	
356	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
357	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
358	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
359	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
360	  \[
361	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
362	  \]
363	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
364	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
365	  isomorphisms down the staircase
366	  \[
367	    (R^n G)(A)
368	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
369	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
370	      \;\cong\; \cdots
371	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
372	  \]
373	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
374	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
375	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
376	  \[
377	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
378	  \]
379	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
380	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
381	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
382	  \[
383	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
384	      \;\cong\;
385	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
386	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
387	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
388	  \]
389	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
390	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
391	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
392	
393	  \emph{Remark on the base case and the Mathlib seed.} The induction reduces every
394	  degree to first-derived functors and cokernels, but the existence and
395	  \(\delta\)-functoriality of the long exact sequence used in
396	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} is what ultimately rests on the
397	  injective-resolution definition of \(R^n G\): the canonical comparison
398	  \(G(J^\bullet) \to RG(A)\) and its degreewise form
399	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong (R^n G)(A)\) are pinned to Mathlib's
400	  \(\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}\) by choosing an injective
401	  resolution \(I^\bullet\) of \(A\), comparing it with the acyclic resolution
402	  \(J^\bullet\) up to homotopy, and using
403	  \ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective} to see that injective objects are
404	  themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic (so the comparison map is a quasi-isomorphism after
405	  applying \(G\)). \emph{(This proof depends on the long exact sequence /
406	  \(\delta\)-functor structure of the right-derived functors \(\{R^k G\}\), which is
407	  the homological infrastructure the dimension shift consumes.)}
408	\end{proof}

57	- The Stacks Project uses the bounded-below derived category $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ throughout, so statements are in that generality. 5594	\begin{lemma}
5595	\label{lemma-F-acyclic}
5596	Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
5597	between abelian categories and assume
5598	$RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
5599	defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
5600	\begin{enumerate}
5601	\item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
5602	$F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
5603	\item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
5604	if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
5605	\end{enumerate}
5606	\end{lemma}
5607	
5608	\begin{proof}
5609	If $A$ is right acyclic for $F$, then $RF(A[0]) = F(A)[0]$ and in
5610	particular $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and
5611	$R^iF(A) = 0$ for $i \not = 0$. Conversely, if $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$
5612	is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$ then
5613	$F(A[0]) \to RF(A[0])$ is a quasi-isomorphism by
5614	Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived} part (1)
5615	and hence $A$ is acyclic. If $F$ is left exact then $F = R^0F$, see
5616	Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived}.
5617	\end{proof}

5619	\begin{lemma}
5620	\label{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}
5621	Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
5622	between abelian categories and assume
5623	$RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
5624	defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
5625	of $\mathcal{A}$.
5626	\begin{enumerate}
5627	\item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
5628	\item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
5629	\item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
5630	surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
5631	\end{enumerate}
5632	In each of the three cases
5633	$$
5634	0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
5635	$$
5636	is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$.
5637	\end{lemma}

5640	By
5641	Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}

5643	$(A[0], B[0], C[0], a, b, c)$ in $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.

5692	\begin{lemma}[Leray's acyclicity lemma]
5693	\label{lemma-leray-acyclicity}
5694	Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
5695	between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
5696	of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
5697	$A^\bullet$\footnote{For example this holds if
5698	$RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere defined.}.
5699	The canonical map
5700	$$
5701	F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
5702	$$
5703	is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
5704	$RF$.
5705	\end{lemma}
5706	
5707	\begin{proof}
5708	Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded complex of right $F$-acyclic objects.
5709	We claim that $RF$ is defined at $A^\bullet$ and that
5710	$F(A^\bullet) \to RF(A^\bullet)$ is an isomorphism in $D^+(\mathcal{B})$.
5711	Namely, it holds for complexes with at most one nonzero right $F$-acyclic
5712	object by Definition \ref{definition-derived-functor}.
5713	Next, suppose that
5714	$A^n = 0$ for $n \not \in [a, b]$. Using the ``stupid'' truncations we obtain
5715	a termwise split short exact sequence of complexes
5716	$$
5717	0 \to \sigma_{\geq a + 1} A^\bullet \to A^\bullet \to
5718	\sigma_{\leq a} A^\bullet \to 0
5719	$$
5720	see
5721	Homology, Section \ref{homology-section-truncations}.
5722	Thus a distinguished triangle
5723	$(\sigma_{\geq a + 1} A^\bullet, A^\bullet, \sigma_{\leq a} A^\bullet)$.
5724	By induction hypothesis $RF$ is defined for the two outer complexes
5725	and these complexes compute $RF$. Then the same is true for the middle one
5726	by Lemma \ref{lemma-2-out-of-3-computes}.
5727	
5728	\medskip\noindent
5729	Suppose that $A^\bullet$ is a bounded below complex of acyclic objects
5730	such that $RF$ is defined at $A^\bullet$.
5731	To show that $F(A^\bullet) \to RF(A^\bullet)$
5732	is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$
5733	it suffices to show that $H^i(F(A^\bullet)) \to H^i(RF(A^\bullet))$
5734	is an isomorphism for
5735	all $i$. Pick $i$. Consider the termwise split short exact sequence of
5736	complexes
5737	$$
5738	0 \to \sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet \to A^\bullet \to
5739	\sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet \to 0.
5740	$$
5741	Note that this induces a termwise split short exact sequence
5742	$$
5743	0 \to \sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet) \to F(A^\bullet) \to
5744	\sigma_{\leq i + 1} F(A^\bullet) \to 0.
5745	$$
5746	Hence we get distinguished triangles
5747	$$
5748	(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet, A^\bullet,
5749	\sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet)
5750	\quad\text{and}\quad
5751	(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet), F(A^\bullet),
5752	\sigma_{\leq i + 1} F(A^\bullet))
5753	$$
5754	Since $RF$ is defined at $A^\bullet$ (by assumption)
5755	and at $\sigma_{\leq i + 1}A^\bullet$ (by the first paragraph)
5756	we see that $RF$ is defined at $\sigma_{\geq i + 2}A^\bullet$
5757	and we get a distinguished triangle
5758	$$
5759	(RF(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} A^\bullet), RF(A^\bullet),
5760	RF(\sigma_{\leq i + 1} A^\bullet))
5761	$$

5763	Using these distinguished triangles we obtain a map of exact sequences
5764	$$
5765	\xymatrix{
5766	H^i(\sigma_{\geq i + 2} F(A^\bullet)) \ar[r] \ar[d] &
5767	H^i(F(A^\bullet)) \ar[r] \ar[d]^\alpha &

5785	\begin{proposition}
5786	\label{proposition-enough-acyclics}
5787	\begin{slogan}
5788	A functor on Abelian categories is extended to the (bounded below or above)
5789	derived category by resolving with a complex that is acyclic for that functor.
5790	\end{slogan}
5791	Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
5792	abelian categories.
5793	\begin{enumerate}
5794	\item If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object acyclic
5795	for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
5796	and we obtain an exact functor
5797	$$
5798	RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
5799	$$
5800	see (\ref{equation-everywhere}). Moreover, any bounded below complex
5801	$A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$.
5802	\item If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ is quotient of
5803	an object acyclic for $LF$, then $LF$ is defined on all of
5804	$K^{-}(\mathcal{A})$ and we obtain an exact functor
5805	$$
5806	LF : D^{-}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{-}(\mathcal{B})
5807	$$
5808	see (\ref{equation-everywhere}). Moreover, any bounded above complex
5809	$A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $LF$ computes $LF$.
5810	\end{enumerate}
5811	\end{proposition}

5813	\begin{proof}
5814	Assume every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object acyclic
5815	for $RF$. Let $\mathcal{I}$ be the set of objects acyclic for $RF$.
5816	Let $K^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex in $\mathcal{A}$. By
5817	Lemma \ref{lemma-subcategory-right-resolution}
5818	there exists a quasi-isomorphism $\alpha : K^\bullet \to I^\bullet$ with
5819	$I^\bullet$ bounded below and $I^n \in \mathcal{I}$. Hence in order to
5820	prove (1) it suffices to show that
5821	$F(I^\bullet) \to F((I')^\bullet)$ is a quasi-isomorphism when
5822	$s : I^\bullet \to (I')^\bullet$ is a quasi-isomorphism of bounded
5823	below complexes of objects from $\mathcal{I}$, see
5824	Lemma \ref{lemma-find-existence-computes}.
5825	Note that the cone $C(s)^\bullet$ is an acyclic bounded below complex
5826	all of whose terms are in $\mathcal{I}$.
5827	Hence it suffices to show: given an acyclic bounded below complex
5828	$I^\bullet$ all of whose terms are in $\mathcal{I}$ the complex
5829	$F(I^\bullet)$ is acyclic.
5830	
5831	\medskip\noindent
5832	Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
5833	$I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
5834	$0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
5835	for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
5836	$(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
5837	Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
5838	For each $k \in \mathbf{Z}$ denote $H_k$ the assertion:
5839	For all $n \leq k$ the object $J^n$ is in $\mathcal{I}$.
5840	Then $H_k$ holds trivially for $k < n_0$. If $H_n$ holds,
5841	then Lemma \ref{lemma-2-out-of-3-computes} shows that
5842	$J^{n + 1}$ is in $\mathcal{I}$ and we have $H_{n + 1}$.
5843	By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
5844	distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.

\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}

The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is
the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the route requires; Mathlib
provides the building blocks (\(\mathrm{InjectiveResolution}\) of an object via enough
injectives) but does not appear to ship the lift itself, so it is recorded here as an
explicit project obligation.

\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
      \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
\end{proof}

\section{The dimension-shift step}

The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
complex-level homology long exact sequence
(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
sub-lemma below.

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
  The short exact sequence \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) induces, via the
  \(\delta\)-functor (long exact sequence) structure of the right-derived functors
  \(\{R^k G\}_{k \geq 0}\), the long exact cohomology sequence in \(\mathcal{B}\)
  \[
    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to (R^1 G)(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^2 G)(A) \to \cdots,
  \]
  where the identifications \(R^0 G(A) = G(A)\), etc., in the first row use the
  left-exactness of \(G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}). Because
  \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term
  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\) vanishes. The segment of the long exact sequence
  around \((R^k G)(J)\) for \(k \geq 1\),
  \[
    (R^{k} G)(J) \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J),
  \]
  then has its two outer terms zero, so the connecting map
  \(\delta : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
  In the lowest degree the segment
  \[
    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) = 0
  \]
  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta\) is an epimorphism with kernel the
  image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta\) identifies the cokernel of
  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is part (2).
\end{proof}

% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the bounded-below derived category
% $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$. Mathlib provides no such delta-functor at the level of the
% right-derived-functor OBJECTS $(R^k G)$, so the proof below is project-original:
% it constructs the same long exact sequence from the complex-level homology long
% exact sequence (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe
% lift (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). No external verbatim proof
% quote is attached, as the route differs from the cited source's.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
  We first manufacture the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\); this is the step that, in the classical
  treatment, is supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the
  derived category, but which we must instead build by hand from the complex-level
  homology long exact sequence.

  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
  complexes
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
  \[
    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
      \longrightarrow 0
  \]
  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
  complex-level homology long exact sequence
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
  \[
    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
  \]
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
  sequence of right-derived functors
  \[
    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
  \]
  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
  \[
    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
  \]

  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
  \[
    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
  \]
  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
  In the lowest degree the segment
  \[
    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
  \]
  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
  part (2).
\end{proof}
